unmapped_findings: The Cu-rich phase in ferrite generally precipitates with BCC structure first and finally transforms into steady-state FCC structure, however, the Cu-rich phase in austenitic precipitates directly in the FCC structure.

According to SAED analysis, the Cu-rich phase has BCC structure (Fig. 1, inset), but IFFT photos show Cu-rich phase crystal plane spacing (00-2) is 0.182 nm which is consistent with Cu in FCC structure.

The lattice parameter of FCC Cu-rich phase and κ-carbide are a = 0.3652 nm and a = 0.3775 nm, respectively. The lattice parameter of austenitic matrix under solution treatment is a = 0.3689 nm obtained by XRD.
The critical lattice parameter of the austenitic matrix is about a = 0.3670 nm.
The lattice parameter of 0Cu steel and 3Cu steel under solid solution treatment are a = 0.3687 nm and a = 0.3689 nm, respectively.

metadata.title: Co-precipitation mechanism of Cu-rich phase and κ-carbide precipitates in Fe-28Mn-10Al-1C-3Cu austenitic low-density steel

others.note: TEM samples with diameter of 3 mm were mechanically polished to about 100 μm thickness, and then twin-jet electro-polished in 10% perchloric acid alcohol

structure.overall_structure: Austenitic matrix with coherent precipitates of κ-carbide and Cu-rich phase

phases_present.grain_size: ~10 nm (diameter for oval), ~30 nm (length for bars)

microstructure_list.defects: almost no dislocations and stacking faults in the Cu-rich phase

precipitates.coherency: coherent with austenitic matrix, lattice misfit 2.3%, interfacial energy 0.025 J/m²
coherent with austenitic matrix, lattice misfit 1.0%, interfacial energy 0.086 J/m²

phases.coherence: coherent, lattice misfit 2.3%, interfacial energy 0.025 J/m²
coherent, lattice misfit 1.0%, interfacial energy 0.086 J/m²

others.precipitation_sequence: κ-carbide precipitates first during aging, followed by Cu-rich phase; precipitation of κ-carbide promotes Cu-rich phase precipitation

interface.phase_evolution: κ-carbide precipitates first during aging treatment, promoting Cu-rich phase precipitation; Cu atoms migrate to austenitic matrix without κ-carbide, increasing Cu concentration near κ-carbide; when Cu concentration reaches critical level, Cu atoms gather and form clusters that precipitate as Cu-rich phase

characterization_methods.purpose: microstructure examination and precipitate morphology observation
characterization_methods.key_findings: Cu-rich phase with FCC structure, irregular oval (~10 nm) and bars shapes (~30 nm), coherent interface with austenite matrix

Fe, Mn and Al atoms exhibited brighter contrast in κ-carbide zone while Cu atoms exhibit darker contrast; Cu atoms exhibit brighter contrast in Cu-rich phase while Fe, Mn, and Al exhibit darker contrast

characterization_methods.purpose: crystal structure determination and orientation relationship analysis
characterization_methods.key_findings: Cu-rich phase has BCC structure in inset of Fig. 1; cube-on-cube orientation relationship between κ-carbide, Cu-rich phase and austenitic matrix
lattice parameter of austenitic matrix under solution treatment is 0.3689 nm; lattice parameters of 0Cu steel and 3Cu steel are 0.3687 nm and 0.3689 nm respectively

analytical_model.parameters: δ = |a₀ - a₁| / a₀, where a₀ and a₁ are lattice parameters
analytical_model.model_note: Calculated lattice misfits: κ-carbide/Cu-rich phase = 3.4%, κ-carbide/austenite = 2.3%, Cu-rich phase/austenite = 1.0%

analytical_model.model_name: Composition boundary formula for κ-carbide precipitation
analytical_model.equation_role: determine critical composition for intragranular κ-carbide precipitation
analytical_model.model_note: Critical Al content for κ-carbide precipitation in 0Cu steel calculated as 6.52 wt%
